title: 第 48 節
en: The objects of knowledge, as given by the Nyaya, comprehend omne scibile, such as body, soul, organs of sense, qualities, cognition, mind, will, fault, death, enjoyment, pain, and final freedom. These objects are afterwards discussed singly, but have of course little to do with logic. Doubt and purpose mark the first steps towards philosophical discussion, instances and established truths supply materials, while premisses and reasoning lead on to the conclusion which disputants wish to reach. From Nos. 10 to 16$ we have rules for dialectic rather than for logic. We are taught how to meet the artifices of our antagonists in a long argumentation, how to avoid or to resist sophistry, wrangling, fallacies, quibbles, false analogies, and downright misstatements, in fact, how to defend truth against unfair antagonists.

If from our point of view we deny the name of logic to such problems, we should be perfectly justified, though a glance at the history of Greek philosophy would show us that, before logic became an independent branch of philosophy it was likewise mixed up with dialectic and with questions of some more special interest, the treatment of which led gradually to the elaboration of general rules of thought, applicable to all reasoning, whatever its subject may be.

It is quite clear that these sixteen topics should on no account be rendered, as they mostly have been, by the sixteen categories. Categories are the praedicabilia, or whatever can be predicated, and however much the meaning of this term may have been varied by European philosophers, it could never have been so far extended as to include wrangling, fallacies, quibbles and all the rest. We shall see that the six or seven Pad&rthas of the Vaiseshikas correspond far more nearly to the categories of the 376 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.
Aristotelian and afterwards of European philosophy in general.
Pad&rtha, Object.

Nothing shows so well the philosophical character of the Sanskrit language as this very word Pad^rtha, which has been translated- by category. It means in ordinary Sanskrit simply a thing, but literally it meant Artha, the meaning, the object, Pada, of a word. What we should call objects of thought, they called far more truly objects of words, thus showing that from the earliest times they understood that no thought was possible except in a word, and that the objects of our knowledge became possible only after they had been named. Their language passed though an opposite process to that of Latin. Latin called every kind of knowledge or all known things gnomina, from g)nosco, to know; but after a time, and after the initial g had been dropped, as we drop it involuntarily in gnat, their gnomina became nominee,, and were then supposed to be something different from the old and forgotten gnomina ; they became nominee, i. e. mere names.
Six Pad&rtlias of Vaiseshika.

According to the Vaiseshikas, we have six Pad&rthas, i.e. six general meanings, categories or predicates, to which all words i.e. all things can be referred. All known things must be either substances (9), qualities (24), or motions, the last meaning, however, more than mere local movement, so as to correspond in fact to our activity or even to our becoming (Werden). Knowledge (Buddhi) is here treated as one of the qualities of the soul, which itself is one of the substances, so that many things which with us belong to psychology and logic, are treated by the Vaiseshikas under this head.
The next two, the general and the particular, comprehend what is shared in common by many objects, and what is peculiar to one, and thus distinguishes it from all others.

Samav&ya or intimate connection is a very useful name for a connection between things which cannot exist one MADEAVAS ACCOUNT OF NYAYA. 377 without the other, such as cause and effect, parts and the whole, and the like. It comes very near to the Avinabhava, i.e. the Not-without-being, and should be carefully distinguished from mere conjunction or succession.
The seventh category, Abhava, or negation, was added, it would seem, at a later time, and can be applied to previous, to present or to subsequent non-existence, or even to absolute Abhava.
M&dhava's Account of Ny&ya.

In order to see what, in the eyes of native scholars, the Nyaya-philosophy was meant to achieve, it may be useful to look at an account of it given by the great Madhava/carya in his Sarvadarsana-samgraha, the compendium of all the systems of philosophy. ' The Nyaya-sastra,' he says, ' consists of five books, and each book contains two daily portions or Ahnikas. In the first Ahnika of the first book the venerable Gotama discusses the definitions of nine subjects, beginning with "proof" (Prama^a), and in the second those of the remaining seven, beginning with discussion (Vada). In the first daily portion of the second book he examines doubt (8), discusses the four kinds of proof, and refutes all objections that could be made against their being considered as instruments of right knowledge ; and in the second he shows that " presumption " and other Prama^as are really included in the four kinds of " proof " already given. In the first daily portion of the third book he examines the soul, the body, the senses, and their objects ; in the second, " understanding " (Buddhi) and mind (Manas). In the first 9daily portion of the fourth book he examines activity (Pravritti), faults (Dosha), transmigration (Pretyabhava), fruit or reward (Phala), pain (DuAkha), and final liberation (Apavarga) ; in the second he investigates the truth as to the causes of the " faults," and also the subject of "wholes" and " parts." In the first daily portion of the fifth book he discusses the various kinds of futility (Crati), and in the second the various kinds of objectionable proceedings (Nigrahasthana)/ After having held out in the first Sutra the promise of eternal salvation to all who study his philosophy properly, INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

Gotama proceeds at once to a description of the steps by which the promised NiAsreyasa, or highest happiness, is to be attained, namely by the successive annihilation of false knowledge, of faults, of activity, and, in consequence, of birth and suffering. * When the last or suffering has been annihilated there follows ipso facto freedom, or blessedness (Apavarga), literally abstersion or purification. This process reminds us strongly of some of the links in the Pa£i/c£a Samuppada of the Buddhists. This is generally translated by Chain of Causation, and was meant to sum up the causes of existence or of misery, the twelve Nidanas. It really means origin resting on something else. The first step is Avidy& or that cosmic Nescience which was so fully elaborated in the Ved&nta-philosophy. According to the Buddhists there follow on Avidy& the Sa??ikharas \ all the varieties of existence ; on these Vi^ana, sensation ; on this N£tmarupa, names and forms; on these the ShacZ&yatana, the six organs of perception. Then follow in succession Sparsa, contact, Vedana, sensation, Trishn&, desire, Up&d&na, attachment, Bhava, state of existence, G&ti, birth, Crar&maraTZ/a, decay and death, $oka, sorrow, Parideva, lamentation-, Du&kha, suffering, Daurmanasya, grief, and Up&y&sa, despair2.

This chain of successive states proclaimed by Buddha has formed the subject of ever so many commentaries, none of which seems quite satisfactory. The chain of Gotarna is shorter than that of Gautama, but the general likeness can hardly be mistaken. Who was the earlier of the two, Gotama or Gautama, is still a contested question, but whatever the age of our Sutras (the sixteen topics) may be, a Ny&ya-philosophy existed clearly before the rise of Buddhism.
I. Fram&Tia.
Gotama proceeds next to examine each of the sixteen topics.
The first topic or Padartha is Prama?ia, which is said to consist of four kinds, all being means or measures of know- 1 Of. Garbe, Samkhya-Philosophie, p. 260 seq .
2 Cf. Childers, s.v.
INFERENCE OB ANUMANA. 379 ledge. They are in the Ny&ya as in the Vaiseshika, (i) Pratyaksha, sense-perception ; (2) Anumana, inference ; (3) Upamana, comparison ; and (4) $abda, word.
Perception or Pratyaksha.

1. Perception (Pratyaksha) is explained as knowledge produced by actual contact between an organ of sense and its corresponding object, this object being supposed to be real. How a mere passive impression, supposing the contiguity of the organs of sense with outward objects had once been established, can be changed into a sensation or into a presentation (Vorstelhmg), or what used to be called a material idea, is a question not even asked by Gotama.
Inference or Anumana.

2. Inference (Anumana), preceded by perception, is described as of three kinds, Purvavat, proceeding from what was before, i. e. an antecedent ; $eshavat, proceeding from what was after, i.e. a consequent; and Samanyato Drishtfa, proceeding from what is constantly seen together. Though, as we saw, .BTarvaka rejects every kind of Anumana or inference, he, as Va&aspati Misra remarks very acutely (K&rika 5), in attacking his antagonists for their mistaken faith in inference, does really himself rely on inference, without which he could not so much as surmise that his antagonists held erroneous opinions, such erroneous opinions being never brought into contact with his organs of sense, but being supposed to exist on the strength of Anumana.

The meaning of the three kinds of inference differs considerably according to different commentators. It is generally explained that a Purvavat, preceded by or possessed of a prius, refers to the mutual relation between a sign and what is signified by it, so that the observation of the sign leads to the observation or rather inference of what is universally associated with it or marked by it. This unconditional association is afterwards treated under the name of Vyapti, literally pervasion of one thing by another. Examples will make this clearer. When we see 380 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

a river rising we infer as its Piirva or prius that it has rained. When we see that the ants carry their eggs, or that the peacocks are screaming, we infer as the $esha or posterior that it will rain (Ny&ya S. II, 5, 37). It is true that in all these cases the reason given for an inference may, as it is called/ wander away, that is, may prove too much or too little. In that case the fault arises from the conditioned character of the Vy&pti or the pervasion. Thus the rising of a river may be due to its having been dammed up, the carrying off their eggs by the ants may have been caused by some accidental disturbance of their hill, and the screaming of the peacocks may really have been imitated by men. The fault, however, in such cases does not affect the process of inference, but the Vyapti only; and as soon as the relation between the sign and the thing signified has been rectified, the inference will come right. Each Vyapti, that is each inductive truth, consists of a sign (Linga), and the bearer of a sign (Lin gin). The bearer of the sign is called Vyapaka or pervading, the sign itself Vy&pya, what is to be pervaded. Thus smoke is the sign (Linga, Vyapya), and fire is what pervades the smoke, is always present when there is smoke, is the sine qua non of smoke, is therefore Lingin or Vy&paka.

But everything depends on whether the two are either absolutely or only conditionally related. These conditions are called the Upadhis. Thus the relation between fire and smoke is conditioned by damp firewood; and there are other cases also where fire exists without smoke, as in a red-hot iron ball.

The third kind of inference, the S&m&nyato Drzshtfa, based on what is constantly seen together, is illustrated by our inferring that the sun is moving because it is seen in different places, everything that is seen in different places being known to have moved. Here the Vy&pti, on which the ancient logicians depended, had to wait till it was corrected by Copernicus.

Even a deaf man may infer the existence of sound if he sees a particular conjunction of a drumstick with a drum. It requires but a certain amount of experience to infer the presence of an ichneumon from seeing an excited snake, or INFERENCE OR ANUMANA. 381 to infer fire from perceiving the heat of water, nay to infer the existence of an organ of touch from our feeling any animated body. In all such cases the correctness of the inference is one thing, the truth of the conclusion quite another, the latter being always conditioned by the presence or absence of certain Upadhis.

Different from this very natural explanation of the three kinds of Anum&na is another, according to which $esha is not supposed to mean subsequent effect, allowing us to infer its invariable cause, but is to be taken in the sense of what is left. This is illustrated by an example, such as f Earth is different from all other elements, because it alone possesses the quality of smell,3 that is to say, earth is left over, being separated from all other elements by its peculiar quality of smell. One might have inferred from the fact that the element of earth possesses smell, that all elements possessed the same. But this is wrong, because it is Aprasakta, i. e. does not apply. It would be no better than if we were to infer that smell must belong to other qualities and actions also, which would be simply absurd. But as earth is different from all other substances, we may infer that smell does not belong to anything that is not earth, except artificially, as in scented articles. This is the residuary inference, or method of residues.
zh: 根據尼耶耶派（Nyaya）的說法，知識的對象包括一切可知之物（omne scibile），例如身體、靈魂、感官、性質、認知、心智、意志、過失、死亡、享樂、痛苦及最終解脫。這些對象在後文逐一討論，但當然與邏輯關係不大。懷疑與目的標示了哲學討論的初始步驟，事例與既定真理提供材料，而前提與推理則引導至爭辯者所欲達成的結論。從第十至十六條，我們所見乃是辯論術的規則，而非邏輯的規則。我們被教導如何在長期的論辯中應對對手的詭計，如何避免或抵禦詭辯、爭執、謬誤、狡辯、虛假類比及明目張膽的歪曲，實際上是如何對抗不公正的對手、捍衛真理。
若從我們的觀點來看，我們否認這類問題具有邏輯的名義，這樣做是完全合理的，儘管瞥一眼希臘哲學史就能告訴我們，在邏輯成為哲學的獨立分支之前，它同樣與辯證法以及某些更具特殊性的問題混雜在一起，對這些問題的探究逐漸導致了通用思維規則的發展，這些規則無論其對象為何，都適用於所有的推理。
很顯然，這十六個主題絕對不應該像大多數人所做的那樣，被轉譯為十六個範疇。範疇是述謂項（praedicabilia），即凡是可被述謂的事物，儘管歐洲哲學家對這一術語的意義有過各種變化，但它永遠不可能被擴展到足以涵括爭執、謬誤、詭辯及諸如此類的事物。我們將看到，毘舍沙迦派（Vaiseshikas）的六、七個實體（Padarthas）在許多方面更接近於範疇。
亞里士多德哲學的，以及隨後歐洲哲學一般的。
帕答他（Padārtha），對象。

沒有什麼比梵文詞彙 Padârtha 更能體現梵文語言的哲學特性。這個詞曾被翻譯為「範疇」。在普通梵文中，它簡單地表示「物」，但字面上它意指 Artha（意義、對象）—— 一個詞的 Pada（詞足）。他們所謂的思想對象，更準確地說是詞的對象，藉此表明從最早的時代起，他們就已領悟到沒有任何思想可能不藉助於詞而產生，而且我們知識的對象也只有在被命名之後才得以可能。他們的語言經歷了與拉丁文相反的過程。拉丁文將各種知識或一切已知之物稱為 gnomina，源自 g)nosco（知道）；但經過一段時間，在初始的 g 被脫落之後——正如我們在 gnat 一詞中無意識地脫落它一樣——他們的 gnomina 變成了 nominâ，而後被認為是有別於那個古老而遺忘的 gnomina 的東西；它們成了 nominâ，亦即純粹的名字。
吠舍沙迦派的六個範疇（Padarthas）

根據勝論派（Vaiseshika）的觀點，我們有六類真實性（Padartha），即六種普遍意義、範疇或謂語，所有的詞語，亦即所有的事物都可以被歸屬於其中。凡是已知的事物必定屬於以下三者之一：實體（9種）、性質（24種）或運動，最後一種意義不止於純粹的局部移動，而是足以對應於我們的活動甚或我們的生成（Werden）。知識（Buddhi）在此被視為靈魂的諸多性質之一，而靈魂本身乃是諸實體之一，因此許多在我們這裡屬於心理學和邏輯學的事物，勝論派卻將其納入這一範疇內加以論述。
接下來的兩個——普遍與特殊——分別指涵蓋許多物體所共有的特性，以及專屬於某一物體的特性，藉此將其與所有其他物體區別開來。
同住（Samavāya）或密切的連繫，是一個非常有用的術語，用來指稱事物之間的一種連繫，即一方離開另一方便無法存在的連繫，如因與果、部分與整體之類。它非常接近「不離在」（Avinabhava），即「不能不存在」的意思，應該與單純的結合或相續小心區別開來。
第七個範疇「阿跋瓦」（Abhava）或否定，似乎是在後來才加入的，可以應用於先前、現在或後續的不存在，甚至可以應用於絕對的阿跋瓦。
摩陀婆論紐雅派

為了瞭解在印度本土學者眼中，尼耶耶哲學（Nyaya-philosophy）旨在達成什麼目標，查閱偉大的摩笯婆遮羅耶（Madhava/carya）在其《諸系統彙編》（Sarvadarsana-samgraha）中對它所作的敘述可能會有所幫助。他說：「尼耶耶經論（Nyaya-sastra）由五部書組成，每一部書包含兩個每日講述部分或稱為『日講』（Ahnikas）。在第一部書的第一個『日講』中，受尊敬的高塔摩（Gotama）論述了九個主題的定義，首先是『証知』（Prama^a），在第二個『日講』中則論述其餘七個的定義，首先是『辯論』（Vada）。在第二部書的第一個每日講述部分中，他考察了懷疑（8），論述了四種証知，並駁斥了所有可能提出反對它們被視為正確知識之工具的異議；在第二個部分中，他證明了『推定』及其他証知實際上已包含在已給出的四種『証知』之中。在第三部書的第一個每日講述部分中，他考察了靈魂、身體、感官及其對象；在第二個部分中考察『理智』（Buddhi）和『心』（Manas）。在第四部書的第一個每日講述部分中，他考察了活動（Pravritti）、缺陷（Dosha）、轉生（Pretyabhava）、果報（Phala）、痛苦（Du Akh a）及最終的解脫（Apavarga）；在第二個部分中，他探究了關於『缺陷』之原因的真理，以及『整體』與『部分』的主題。在第五部書的第一個每日講述部分中，他論述了各種『徒勞』（Crati），在第二個部分中論述了各種『應受譴責的進行方式』（Nigrahasthana）。在第一部經文中許諾永恆救贖給所有適當研習其哲學者之後，

喬達摩立即著手描述達到所許諾的涅槃喜樂（NiAsreyasa），即最高幸福的各個步驟，亦即通過逐次消滅虛假知識、過失、業行，以及因此所帶來的生與苦而達成此目標。*當最後的苦被消滅後，隨之自然而然地產生自由或福樂（Apavarga），字面意為淨化或潔淨。這一過程令人強烈想起佛教徒的帕利文《因緣經》(Pa£i/c£a Samuppada)中的某些環節。這通常被譯為「因果鏈」，旨在總括存在或苦難的原因，即十二因緣。它實際上意指根植於他物的起源。第一步乃為阿毗達（Avidy&），即那在吠陀哲學中充分闡明的宇宙無明。根據佛教徒的說法，在阿毗達之後相繼而來的是行（Sa??ikharas），即存在的各種形態；其後是識（Vi^ana），感覺；其後是名色（N£tmarupa），名與色；其後是六入（ShacZ&yatana），六根。接著依次而來的是觸（Sparsa）、受（Vedana）、感覺、渴愛（Trishn&）、慾望、取（Up&d&na）、執著、有（Bhava）、存在的狀態、生（G&ti）、出生、老死（Crar&maraTZ/a）、衰亡與死亡、憂（$oka）、哀（Parideva）、悲、苦（Du&kha）、苦悶（Daurmanasya）、愁與絕望（Up&y&sa）²。
佛陀所宣說的這一連串相繼狀態，成為無數註疏的主題，其中似乎沒有一部完全令人滿意。高塔爾納的鏈條比喬達摩的要短，但其整體相似性幾乎不容誤認。誰是較早的人物——高塔爾納還是喬達摩——仍是一個爭議的問題，但無論我們的經典（十六個論題）的時代為何，彌曼差哲學明確地在佛教興起之前就已存在。
I. 弗拉姆與蒂亞
喬達摩接下來逐一檢視這十六個主題。
第一個主題或範疇（Padartha）是量論（Pramāṇa），據說包含四種，全部皆為認知的手段或量度。
2 參考《巴利語–英語辭典》（Childers），相關詞條。
推理或阿奴摩那。379 識。在尼耶亞學派（Nyaya）與瓦伊舍希卡學派（Vaiseshika）中皆同，分為：（一）現量知（Pratyaksha），感官知覺；（二）推理（Anumana），推論；（三）比量知（Upamana），比較；及（四）聲量知（Sabda），言語。
知覺或直觀知識（Pratyaksha）。
知覺（Pratyaksha）被闡釋為由感官與其對應對象的實際接觸所產生的知識，此對象被假定為真實的。至於單純的被動印象——假設感官與外部對象的接近一旦確立——如何能被轉變為感覺或為表象（Vorstellung），或曾被稱為物質觀念（material idea）的東西，這甚至不是瞿曇（Gotama）所提問的問題。
推論或阿奴瑪那（Anumana）。

推論（Anumana）以知覺為先導，被描述為三種類型：前因推論（Purvavat），由先前之物推進，即前行因；後果推論（Sheshavat），由後來之物推進，即後續果；以及共同所見推論（Samanyato Drishtia），由常見相伴之物推進。雖然如我們所見，*Charvaka* 派否定一切推論（Anumana）或推理，但如瓦查斯帕蒂‧密什拉（Vacaspati Misra）在其《卡里卡》（Karika）第五條所尖銳地論述的那樣，他在攻擊對手對推論的錯誤信仰時，實際上卻本身依賴推論。若無推論，他根本無法推測其對手持有謬誤的觀點，因為這些謬誤的觀點從未與其感覺器官相接觸，而係被假定存在於推論（Anumana）的基礎上。
三種推論的涵義根據不同的評論家而有相當大的差異。通常的解釋是，先有式（Purvavat）即先行者或具有先行者之物，指的是標記與被其所標記之物之間的相互關係，因此對標記的觀察導致對普遍與其相關聯或被其所標記之物的觀察，或更確切地說，是對其的推論。這種無條件的關聯後來在遍連（Vyapti），字面上意為一物被另一物所遍佈的名稱下得到處理。例子將使這一點更加清晰。當我們看到 380 印度哲學。

一條河水上漲，我們推斷其**先行因**（*Pūrva*或*prius*）為下雨了。當我們看到螞蟻搬運卵粒，或孔雀在尖叫時，我們推斷其**後續果**（*śeṣa*或*posterior*）為將要下雨（《因明論》第二卷第五章第三十七節）。誠然，在所有這些情形中，為推論而給出的理由可能如其名稱所謂的會「遊蕩」，亦即可能過度推論或推論不足。在此情況下，過失源於**周遍**（*vyāpti*）之條件性特質。河水上漲可能是因為被堵截了，螞蟻搬運卵粒可能是由於其蟻丘遭到某種偶發擾動，孔雀的尖叫實際上可能是人類模仿的。然而，這類情況中的過失並不影響推論的過程本身，只影響**周遍**（*vyāpti*）；一旦**標記**與**所標記物**之間的關係得以修正，推論便會成立。每一**周遍**（*vyāpti*），即每一歸納真理，皆由**標記**（*Liṅga*）及**標記之承體**（*Liṅgin*）所組成。標記之承體稱為**周遍者**（*vyāpaka*）或遍佈者，標記本身稱為**所周遍**（*vyāpya*），即待被遍佈者。是故煙是**標記**（*Liṅga*、*vyāpya*），火是遍佈煙者，總是在有煙之處存在，是煙的**必要條件**（*sine qua non*），因此是**標記之承體**（*Liṅgin*）或**周遍者**（*vyāpaka*）。
但一切取決於這兩者是絕對相關，還是僅有條件地相關。這些條件被稱為烏帕迪（Upadhis）。因此火與煙之間的關係是以濕木柴為條件的；還有其他情況，火存在而沒有煙，如一個燒紅的鐵球。
第三種推理——同時所見（Sāmānyato Dṛśṭvā）——係基於經常相伴而觀察到的事物。舉例而言，我們推論太陽在運動，因為在不同的地方都看得到它，而凡是在不同地方看得到的事物，都已知是有運動的。古代邏輯學家所依據的這種周遍（Vyāpti），必須等到哥白尼的修正才得以成立。
即使是聾子，若見到鼓槌與鼓的特定結合，也可推斷聲音的存在。只需具有一定的經驗，就足以從看到蛇的激動狀態推斷出獴的存在，或**推論（anumāna）**；從感知水的熱度推斷火的存在；甚至從感覺任何有生命的身體推斷出觸覺器官的存在。在所有這類情況下，推論的正確性是一回事，結論的真理性完全是另一回事，後者始終受制於某些因素（Upadhis）的有無。

與此三種因明（Anumāna）的非常自然的解釋不同的，還有另一種解釋，根據這種解釋，*Śeṣa* 不是指推論其不變之原因的後果，而應理解為「剩餘物」的意思。這可以用以下例子說明：「地不同於其他所有元素，因為唯有地具有氣味的特質，」也就是說，地被剩餘下來，因其特有的氣味特質而與所有其他元素區分開。人們本可能從地元素具有氣味這一事實推論出所有元素都具有同樣的特質。但這是錯誤的，因為這是*Aprasakta*，即不適用。這不比如果我們推論氣味必定也屬於其他特質和作用同樣糟糕，那純粹是荒謬的。但因為地不同於所有其他物質，我們可以推論氣味不屬於任何非地的物質，除非是人為地製造的，如同芳香製品。這就是剩餘推論，或稱殘差法（method of residues）。
